preamble: Labial palatalization, the shift from a voiceless labial stop [p] to a voiceless palatal stop [c], is a cross-linguistically rarer process but well-documented in several language families. This fronting or 'palatalizing' of labials typically occurs before high front vowels or glides, and is a noted feature of many Bantu languages and specific dialects of Greek.

examples.note: Bantu languages like Zulu or Tswana exhibit instances where labials shift to palatals (e.g., passive formations).
Certain dialects of Modern Greek show the shift of labials to palatals in specific phonetic environments.
The Latin cluster /pj/ often develops into a palatal stop in Southern Italian varieties.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
